elements.sent-2411-001: Johnson$^{1}$, Max Katz$^{1}$, Alexander Smith Clark$^{1}$} \ead{michael.zingale@stonybrook.edu.
elements.sent-2411-002: Stellar evolution is driven by the changing composition of a star from nuclear reactions.
elements.sent-2411-003: At the late stages of evolution and during explosive events, the timescale can be short and drive strong hydrodynamic flows, making simulations of astrophysical reacting flows challenging.
elements.sent-2411-004: Over the past decades, the standard approach to modeling reactions in simulation codes has been operator splitting, using implicit integrators for reactions.
elements.sent-2411-005: Here we explore some of the assumptions in this standard approach and describe some techniques for improving the efficiency and accuracy of astrophysical reacting flows.
elements.sent-2411-006: The work at Stony Brook was supported by DOE/Office of Nuclear Physics grant DE-FG02-87ER40317 and a seed-funding award from the Stony Brook University Office of the Vice President for Research.
elements.sent-2411-007: This research used resources of the National Energy Research Scientific Computing Center (NERSC), a Department of Energy Office of Science User Facility using NERSC award NP-ERCAP0027167.
elements.sent-2411-008: This research used resources of the Oak Ridge Leadership Computing Facility at the Oak Ridge National Laboratory, which is supported by the Office of Science of the U.S.
elements.sent-2411-009: Department of Energy under Contract No. DE-AC05-00OR22725.. \bibliographystyle{iopart-num.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Many stellar phenomena are driven by the interplay of nuclear reactions and hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-011: During the different phases of stellar evolution, the behavior of reaction-driven convection, mixing at composition interfaces, and the feedback between explosive energy release and hydrodynamic flow all strongly depend on modeling the reactions accurately.
elements.sent-2411-012: Thermonuclear explosions in nova depend on resolving the interface between the white dwarf and accreted layer, allowing CNO elements to mix and catalyze the burning.
elements.sent-2411-013: X-ray burst (XRB) flames in mixed H/He layers require a large number of nuclei to accurately capture the flame speed.
elements.sent-2411-014: Type Ia supernova, in all the various progenitor models, involves burning fronts that intimately connect hydrodynamics and reactions.
elements.sent-2411-015: The convective burning shells in the late stages of massive star evolution involve mixing between the shells and require large reaction networks to capture the core evolution.
elements.sent-2411-016: All of these systems provide challenges for multidimensional models.
elements.sent-2411-017: Numerical simulations of these environments require algorithms that strongly couple the reactions and the hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-018: Operator splitting methods, commonly used in astrophysics, can break down \cite{sportisse:2000 , and the common workaround of simply cutting the timestep or disabling the burning (see, e.g., \cite{zingale:2001,Fields_2020,rivas:2022 ) is not efficient or accurate.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Instead, we focus on methods that strongly couple hydrodynamics and reactions, allowing the reactions to “see” the hydrodynamic flow and respond immediately as the burning proceeds.
elements.sent-2411-020: Over the past few years, we have been developing such methods for astrophysical flows, based on the ideas of spectral deferred corrections, and have shown that strongly-coupled time-integration methods can be more efficient than operator splitting on a variety of problems.
elements.sent-2411-021: Here we review our algorithmic development for modeling astrophysical reacting flows, discuss the application to various astrophysical environments, and describe our success at utilizing GPUs to efficiently model these events.
elements.sent-2411-022: The \amrex-Astrophysics suite \cite{astronum:2017 is a collection of open-source astrophysics simulation codes built around the \amrex\ adaptive mesh library \cite{amrex_joss , and includes the compressible hydrodynamics code \castro~\cite{castro,castro_joss , the low Mach number hydrodynamics code \maestroex~\cite{maestroex , and the cosmology code \nyx~\cite{nyx .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: These codes are designed for exascale computers and to be highly performance-portable, making excellent use of GPUs \cite{castro_gpu .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Our approach to using GPUs is to allocate the state data directly on the GPU at the start of the simulation and then run all of the physics on the GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-025: To enable this, we utilize the AMReX C++-lambda-capturing mechanism to offload a kernel to GPUs using CUDA (for NVIDIA GPUs) or HIP (for AMD GPUs).
elements.sent-2411-026: In our adaptive mesh hierarchy, each grid is assigned to an MPI task / GPU, and then each of the zones in that grid are mapped to a single GPU compute core.
elements.sent-2411-027: This exposes a lot of parallelism and allows us to run an order-of-magnitude faster using GPUs vs.\ CPUs on a typical supercomputer node.
elements.sent-2411-028: To enable this performance-portable approach to GPUs, we took on the task of porting the code to C++.
elements.sent-2411-029: Figure~\ref{fig:castro shows the history of this transition, where were were able to move pieces of the code at a time to C++ while maintaining a functioning code.}
elements.sent-2411-030: This porting paid off, with \castro\ able to strong-scale to $\mathcal{O (10^3)$ nodes on supercomputers, as shown in the second panel of Figure~\ref{fig:castro .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: For reactions, our GPU approach means that the entire ODE integration is managed on GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-032: This can cause issues with thread-divergence if one zone takes considerably more time than others---we will discuss some ways of mitigating this below.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.45\linewidth]{lang.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.45\linewidth]{frontier_flame_wave_scaling.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:castro} (left) the evolution of the programming languages used in \castro, showing the sharp decline in Fortran to the fully C++ codebase today (right) strong scaling for a 3D XRB flame (from \cite{castro-3d-xrb}) on the OLCF Summit (6 GPUs / node) and Frontier (8 GPUs / node) machines. We see good scaling to $\mathcal{O}(10^3)$ nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Significant effort has been made by our group to accurately model reactive flows in stars, going beyond the standard operator-splitting techniques widely used in the field.
elements.sent-2411-034: We can write the equations of reactive flow as a system in conservative form: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \Uc}{\partial t} = \Advs{\Uc} + \Rb(\Uc)\end{equation} where $\Uc$ represents the conserved state of the fluid (density, momentum, energy), $\Advs{\Uc}$ is the hydrodynamic advection term, and $\Rb(\Uc)$ are the reactive sources.
elements.sent-2411-035: In an operator-split approach to coupling reactions and hydrodynamics, we treat the advection and reactions independent of one-another.
elements.sent-2411-036: In \castro, the $\Advs{\Uc}$ term is computed using a unsplit piecewise parabolic method \cite{ppmunsplit,ppm .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The splitting means that when we integrate the reaction term, there is no energy / temperature change due to hydrodynamic expansion / compression.
elements.sent-2411-038: In flows where the burning is strongly influencing the flow, like detonations, this means that we can lose order-of-accuracy or get the nucleosynthesis wrong unless we cut the timestep (see, e.g., \cite{strang_rnaas,castro_simple_sdc ).}
elements.sent-2411-039: Recently we introduced the ``simplified-SDC'' time-integration method in \castro~\cite{castro_simple_sdc , based on the traditional spectral deferred corrections approach \cite{dutt:2000,bourlioux:2003,castro-sdc that we explored earlier.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The simplified SDC method updates the conserved state by evolving the reaction ODEs with an explicit advection term: \begin{equation}\frac{d\Uc}{dt} = \Adv{\Uc}^{n+1/2} + \Rb(\Uc)\end{equation} where the advective term, $\Adv{\Uc ^{n+1/2}$, is constructed at the midpoint in time using characteristic tracing and treated as piecewise-constant-in-time during the reaction evolution.}
elements.sent-2411-041: The result is that reactions explicitly sees what advection does over a timestep.
elements.sent-2411-042: This allows us to integrate explosive reacting flows without having to cut the timestep below the hydrodynamics timestep.
elements.sent-2411-043: All of these time-integration methods are built into \castro.
elements.sent-2411-044: The \amrex-Astrophysics codes also share a common set of microphysics (which we refer to as \amrex-Astro \microphysics\footnote{\url{https://github.com/amrex-astro/Microphysics ).}}
elements.sent-2411-045: This provides equations of state, reaction networks, transport coefficients, and integrators that can handle both operator-split and SDC time-integration methods.
elements.sent-2411-046: For reaction networks, we are tightly integrated with \pynucastro \cite{pynucastro2 , which allows us to generate a custom C++ reaction network for any science application with just a few lines of python.}
elements.sent-2411-047: All of the networks and integrators are written in a manner that they can be easily offloaded to GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-048: In the next sections, we describe some of the choices commonly made in simulations of astrophysical reacting flows and look at whether alternatives to the standard approaches can be viable in some situations.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Choice of integrator for stiff reaction networks
elements.sent-2411-049: Reaction networks are evolved by solving a system of ordinary differential equations (ODEs), with the integrator taking many small timesteps to achieve the desired output time.
elements.sent-2411-050: Historically for astrophysical reaction networks, simple backward-Euler or Crank-Nicolson integrators have been used \cite{arnetttruran:1969,woosley:1973,prometheus,hixmeyer:2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Ideally, the timestep will be controlled by specifying integrator tolerances and monitoring truncation error, but sometimes heuristics are used \cite{hixmeyer:2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: An extensive study of integrators (and linear algebra packages) was done by \cite{timmes:1999 and explored a 4th-order Kaps-Rentrop method and a variable order Bader-Deuflhard method, both of which provide error estimates, and found that the higher order methods are more efficient and accurate.}
elements.sent-2411-053: These are the basis for the networks in \flash~\cite{flash .}
elements.sent-2411-054: The benefits of high-order integration methods were also explored in \cite{longland:2014 , which also concluded that they perform better than low order methods on a variety of problems.}
elements.sent-2411-055: In our \amrex-Astro \microphysics\ package, we use the variable order, backward difference implicit VODE integrator \cite{vode as the default.}
elements.sent-2411-056: VODE is widely used in the chemical combustion community (e.g.\ PeleC~\cite{PeleC ), and a C port is also included in the SUNDIALS library~\cite{sundials .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: In \microphysics, we ported the original VODE Fortran 77 source to a templated C++ header library, and added some special step rejection logic that knows about mass fractions (see \cite{castro_simple_sdc ).}
elements.sent-2411-058: This port allows us to offload the entire ODE integration onto GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-059: All of these implicit integrators require a Jacobian, which is either evaluated analytically or via finite-differencing.
elements.sent-2411-060: Carrying the Jacobian takes memory (sparse storage helps \cite{timmes:1999 ) and expensive linear algebra operations are needed to advance the state.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Sometimes Jacobian caching is used, where the same Jacobian may be reused for multiple steps (if the change in solution is small) to reduce the computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-062: However, we disable Jacobian caching when running on GPUs due to the additional memory needed.
elements.sent-2411-063: The analytic Jacobian may be approximated, for instance, by ignoring the composition dependence in the plasma state for screening or in compound approximation rates (like those used in {\tt aprox19 \cite{Kepler ).}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Sometimes the approximate analytic Jacobian works well, but when it doesn't, the integrator can fail.
elements.sent-2411-065: In these instances, the numerical Jacobian often works.
elements.sent-2411-066: In \microphysics, we allow for a zone to retry the burn if it fails, swapping the Jacobian type for the retry.
elements.sent-2411-067: This proves to be surprisingly robust.
elements.sent-2411-068: At high temperatures, forward and reverse reaction rates can balance and the nuclei enter into nuclear statistical equilibrium (NSE).
elements.sent-2411-069: This can be challenging for integrators to model, since the forward and reverse rates can both be large and need to cancel out to high precision in order to maintain equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-070: Integrators that focus on the quasi-steady state behavior \cite{Mott:2000,Guidry:2013 can help in some situations, but do not seem to have been widely adopted in astrophysics.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Methods which detect when the reactive flow enters into NSE have been used for thermonuclear supernovae \cite{kushnirkatz:2020 as have heuristic-based approaches to assuming NSE \cite{ma:2013,bravo:2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-072: We have both approaches implemented in \microphysics.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Example: massive stars and nuclear statistical equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-073: For the evolution of the iron core in massive stars nearing core-collapse, we want to understand the NSE state from 100s of nuclei, including electron/positron captures/decays.
elements.sent-2411-074: This can be accomplished by tabulating the NSE state and electron fraction evolution in terms of density, temperature, and electron fraction, and using this NSE state to compute the energy release and evolution.
elements.sent-2411-075: NSE tables have been used with operator splitting for thermonuclear supernova for some time \cite{townsley:2007,seitenzhal:2009,ma:2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: In the simplified-SDC framework, it is possible to capture the time-evolution of the NSE state in a second-order accurate fashion, using a predictor-corrector approach, as we demonstrated in \cite{sdc-nse .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{all_massive_star_plt27739_w0.15_vertical} \caption{\label{fig:massivestar} Mach number, vorticity, mean molecular weight, and energy generation rate for a $15~M_\odot$ star in the inner 15\% of the simulation domain. The black contour in each panel shows the boundary between when we assume NSE and when we integrate the network.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Figure~\ref{fig:massivestar shows a snapshot of the convective flow field near the core of a massive star ($15~M_\odot$).}
elements.sent-2411-078: As a fluid element enters the core, we switch from integrating a 19-isotope network to using the NSE table to give us the evolution of the electron fraction of the state (due to weak reactions).
elements.sent-2411-079: The NSE table also maps the $\mathcal{O (100)$ nuclei to the 19 we carry on the grid, allowing us continue to track the fluid element if it leaves the core.}
elements.sent-2411-080: With the simplified-SDC time-integration approach, we are able to evolve on the hydrodynamic timestep, without needing to cut the timestep to the much smaller nuclear timestep (as is done in other work).
elements.sent-2411-081: Furthermore, in contrast to most other works which cutout \cite{muller:2017,yadav:2020,varmamueller:2023 or approximate the core \cite{yoshida:2019,yoshida-apj:2021,fields:2020 , the NSE treatment allows us to include the entire core self-consistently.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The burning in the Si layer nearing NSE is difficult and the integrator can require a lot of internal steps.
elements.sent-2411-083: We found however that if we put a low cap on the number of ODE integration timesteps ($< 5000$), and allow the burn to fail, and then retry with a different Jacobian approximation, the massive star runs $1.7\times$ faster in 3D on the AMD GPUs on OLCF Frontier.
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Example: X-ray bursts and explicit integrators.
elements.sent-2411-084: In chemical combustion, \cite{niemeyersung showed that stabilized explicit integrators, like Runge-Kutta-Chebyshev (RKC) \cite{rkc , can be more efficient than implicit integrators for moderately-stiff reaction networks, especially on GPUs.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: These have the advantage that a Jacobian is not needed---only an estimate of the spectral radius is required.
elements.sent-2411-086: We ported the original Fortran implementation of RKC to C++ in \microphysics, and are able to use it for any of our networks.
elements.sent-2411-087: In \cite{johnson:2023 , we looked at the performance of RKC for flames in XRBs and found that in some situations, these flames can be modeled more efficiently with the explicit RKC integrator, compared to VODE.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The benefits of the explicit integrator are problem-dependent.
elements.sent-2411-089: Recently we have been exploring ``layered'' models of XRBs (a pure He layer beneath a H layer) \cite{xrb_layered .}
elements.sent-2411-090: For our latest 3D simulation of layered XRBs (see figure~\ref{fig:xrb_layered ), we found that RKC is almost twice as fast as the implicit VODE integrator with a 21-isotope H-burning network for the initial evolution (as long as the temperature is less than $10^9~\mathrm{K}$).}
elements.sent-2411-091: This suggests that a future optimization will be to use different integrators for different thermodynamic conditions in a single simulation.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{slice_xrb_plt4528578} \caption{\label{fig:xrb_layered} Slice through a 3D simulation of layered convection in an XRB. On the left is vorticity, showing the convective field in the main fuel layer. Only the lower part of the domain is shown---the region above the fuel layer is low density material intended to give the atmosphere room to expand as it heats up. On the right is the energy generation rate, showing two distinct layers: He burning beneath H burning.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Most astrophysical simulation codes use operator splitting (in particular Strang splitting \cite{strang:1968 ) to couple hydrodynamics and reactions.}
elements.sent-2411-093: This goes back at least to the prometheus code \cite{prometheus .}
elements.sent-2411-094: Futhermore, the importance of evolving an energy, temperature, or entropy equation along with the rates was noted by several groups \cite{muller:1986,prometheus,cabezon:2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-095: In some codes (e.g.\ \cite{flash ), the splitting is further approximated by keeping the temperature constant during the integration, which we showed can drop the overall reactive-hydrodynamics evolution to first-order accurate \cite{strang_rnaas .}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Difficulties in coupling hydrodynamics and reactions when doing operator splitting often requires cutting the timestep.
elements.sent-2411-097: This is commonly needed for modeling detonations in the double detonation progenitor model for Type Ia supernovae (SN Ia).
elements.sent-2411-098: In our studies of the double detonation SN Ia problem \cite{castro-sndd , we found that the simplified-SDC integration is more accurate than Strang splitting and also computationally less expensive.}
elements.sent-2411-099: When the reaction integration includes the advective terms, it seems to remove some of the stiffness from the problem---we discussed this earlier in \cite{astronum:2019 , where we showed that the simplified-SDC integration evaluates the righthand side of the reaction ODE system fewer times than the Strang split case when modeling a detonation.}
elements.sent-2411-100: There are several different approaches to offloading reactions to GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-101: The Sundials library \cite{sundials runs the integrator on the CPU and launches GPU kernels to operate on the state held in GPU memory.}
elements.sent-2411-102: This is done to allow for a batched integration strategy in the adaptive timestepping algorithm, where a large number of zones are advanced with the same timestep.
elements.sent-2411-103: This can reduce thread divergence on GPUs and could be of benefit for some of our applications.
elements.sent-2411-104: Our approach matches more with that of \cite{stonedavis:2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-105: We do the entire ODE integration on the GPU.
elements.sent-2411-106: Our kernel is the entire VODE or RKC integrator along with our network-specific righthand side and Jacobian routines and equation of state.
elements.sent-2411-107: As discussed with massive stars, one way to help with thread divergence is to put a cap on the number of ODE integration steps used by the reaction integrator.
elements.sent-2411-108: If this fails, then we can force a retry at the application level, discarding the entire AMR level advance and redoing it with a smaller timestep.
elements.sent-2411-109: We discussed some recent developments in modeling reactive flows in stars.
elements.sent-2411-110: For many problems, exploring alternate time-integration strategies that traditionally done in astrophysics can lead to better performance and more accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-111: All of the developments we discuss here are freely available in the \amrex-Astro code github repositories.
